subject: biology
ncdc_competency: The learner assesses the physiological and behavioural processes during organisms' responses to internal and external stimuli, in order to appreciate the adaptive behaviour of organisms and, therefore, promote their welfare to ensure survival and reproductive success.

content.body: Plants cannot move but they respond to stimuli through growth movements called tropisms and through hormonal regulation. These responses allow plants to maximise photosynthesis, anchor roots, and time flowering — all critical for agricultural productivity in Uganda.
Tropisms

items: Phototropism: growth response to light. Shoots are positively phototropic (grow toward light); roots are negatively phototropic (grow away from light).
Geotropism (gravitropism): growth response to gravity. Roots are positively geotropic (grow downward); shoots are negatively geotropic (grow upward).
Thigmotropism: growth response to touch (e.g. climbing plants like passion fruit wrap tendrils around supports).
Hydrotropism: growth of roots toward water — important in dry Ugandan soils.

content.body: Auxins (IAA — Indole Acetic Acid)
Auxins are produced in the shoot apex (growing tip) and move downward. They promote cell elongation in stems but inhibit elongation in roots (roots are much more sensitive to auxin).

content.heading: How auxins explain phototropism (Cholodny-Went hypothesis):
items: Light hits one side of the shoot tip.
Auxins redistribute — migrate to the shaded side.
The shaded side has more auxin → cells on the shaded side elongate more.
The shoot bends toward the light (differential growth).
In roots: the same redistribution occurs, but roots inhibit on the high-auxin side → root bends away from light.

content.heading: Commercial uses of auxins in Uganda:
items: Rooting powder (IBA — indole butyric acid): applied to cuttings of crops like cassava and tea to stimulate adventitious root formation.
Herbicides: synthetic auxins (e.g. 2,4-D) at high concentrations kill broad-leaved weeds in maize fields without harming the grass-like maize.
Fruit development: applied to flowers to develop fruits without pollination (parthenocarpy) — important in seedless fruit production.

content.body: Other Plant Hormones

items: Gibberellins: promote stem elongation (cell elongation AND cell division), seed germination (break dormancy — activate amylase to digest seed reserves), and fruit development. Commercial use: brewing industry (stimulates barley germination for malt production); sugarcane cultivation (increases internode length).

Cytokinins: promote cell division (cytokinesis). Delay leaf senescence (ageing). Work with auxins to control growth — high cytokinin:auxin ratio promotes shoot growth; high auxin:cytokinin ratio promotes root growth. Commercial use: tissue culture for crop propagation.

Abscisic acid (ABA): stress hormone. Promotes stomatal closure during drought (as covered in Topic 2). Promotes seed dormancy. Promotes leaf and fruit abscission (dropping). Also called the 'stress hormone'.

Ethylene (ethene): a gas hormone. Promotes fruit ripening. Promotes leaf, flower, and fruit abscission. Commercial use: applied to bananas, tomatoes, and avocados to trigger uniform ripening before market — critical for Uganda's fruit export industry.

content.body: Photoperiodism: Controlling Flowering

Photoperiodism is the response of a plant to the relative lengths of day and night. Plants use this mechanism to time their flowering to the correct season. The critical factor is actually the length of the DARK period (night), not the light period.

content.heading: Types of photoperiodic plants:
items: Short-day plants (long-night plants): flower when the dark period exceeds a critical minimum length (i.e. days are short). Examples: chrysanthemum, rice, strawberry. Flower in short days of the wet season in Uganda.

Long-day plants (short-night plants): flower when the dark period is shorter than a critical maximum (i.e. days are long). Examples: wheat, lettuce, carnation. Flower in long days of temperate summers.
Day-neutral plants: flowering not controlled by day length — controlled by temperature or age. Examples: maize, tomato, cucumber — common in Uganda.

content.heading: Application in floriculture (flower farming):
items: Floriculture farms (e.g. in Entebbe, Uganda) manipulate day length artificially to control flowering time for export markets.
Giving chrysanthemums artificially long nights (covering with black plastic): induces flowering outside the natural season.
Using supplementary lighting to create short nights: delays flowering — maintains vegetative growth.

questions.question: A maize farmer in Uganda applies 2,4-D herbicide to a field. Broad-leaved weeds die but the maize is unaffected. The reason 2,4-D kills the weeds is that it:
options: Is a cytokinin that prevents weed cell division
Is a synthetic auxin that at high concentrations causes uncontrolled growth in broad-leaved plants, disrupting normal development
Blocks photosynthesis in all plants except grasses
Inhibits gibberellin production in weeds only
questions.answer: Is a synthetic auxin that at high concentrations causes uncontrolled growth in broad-leaved plants, disrupting normal development
questions.explanation: 2,4-D is a synthetic auxin analogue. At high concentrations it mimics the effects of excessive auxin, causing uncontrolled, disorganised growth in broad-leaved plants (dicots) that kills them. Monocots like maize are relatively resistant due to differences in auxin metabolism.

questions.question: A student places a seedling horizontally in the dark. After 24 hours, the shoot curves upward and the root curves downward. This is best explained by:
options: Light stimulating upward growth in the shoot
Auxin redistribution due to gravity — higher auxin on the lower side promotes shoot elongation but inhibits root elongation
Gibberellins promoting downward root growth only
Cytokinin promoting cell division in the upper side of the shoot
questions.answer: Auxin redistribution due to gravity — higher auxin on the lower side promotes shoot elongation but inhibits root elongation
questions.explanation: Gravity causes auxin to accumulate on the lower side of both shoot and root. In the shoot, higher auxin on the lower side promotes more elongation there, bending the shoot upward (negative geotropism). In the root, the same higher auxin on the lower side inhibits elongation (roots are more sensitive), so the upper side elongates more and the root curves downward (positive geotropism).

questions.question: A banana ripening facility in Kampala uses ethylene gas to ripen green bananas uniformly before sending them to market. Which other natural process does ethylene regulate in plants?
options: Seed germination and dormancy breaking
Leaf and fruit abscission (dropping)
Root formation from cuttings
Stomatal opening during daylight
questions.explanation: Ethylene promotes fruit ripening AND controls abscission — the controlled dropping of leaves, flowers, and fruits. It stimulates the formation of an abscission zone at the base of the leaf or fruit stalk, where cells separate. This is why ripe fruits drop naturally and leaves fall in deciduous trees.

questions.question: A floriculture farmer in Entebbe wants chrysanthemums (short-day plants) to flower outside their normal season. To trigger flowering, the farmer should:
options: Expose plants to continuous light for 24 hours
Cover plants with black plastic to artificially extend the dark period beyond the critical night length
Apply gibberellins to promote stem elongation
Reduce watering to trigger ABA-induced flowering
questions.answer: Cover plants with black plastic to artificially extend the dark period beyond the critical night length
questions.explanation: Short-day (long-night) plants flower when the continuous dark period exceeds their critical night length. By covering plants with black plastic, the farmer artificially extends the night, triggering the flowering response regardless of the actual calendar date — allowing year-round production for export markets.

questions.question: Abscisic acid (ABA) is called the 'stress hormone' of plants. During a drought, ABA has which IMMEDIATE effect on leaves?
options: Promotes cell division to produce new leaves
Causes stomatal closure to reduce water loss by transpiration
Stimulates root elongation toward water
Activates gibberellins to break seed dormancy
questions.answer: Causes stomatal closure to reduce water loss by transpiration
questions.explanation: During water stress, ABA is synthesised in leaf mesophyll cells and transported to guard cells. It triggers efflux of K⁺ ions from guard cells → water potential rises in guard cells → water leaves by osmosis → guard cells become flaccid → stomata close. This immediately reduces transpiration water loss.

content.body: Structure of a Neurone

content.heading: Parts of a motor neurone:
items: Cell body (soma): contains nucleus and most organelles.
Dendrites: short, branched extensions that receive impulses from other neurones or receptors.
Axon: long single fibre that carries impulses away from the cell body.
Myelin sheath: formed by Schwann cells wrapping around the axon. Made of lipid — acts as electrical insulator, greatly increasing conduction speed.
Nodes of Ranvier: gaps in the myelin sheath where the axon is exposed. Impulse 'jumps' from node to node — saltatory conduction.
Synaptic knobs (terminal boutons): swellings at the end of the axon where neurotransmitters are stored in vesicles.

content.body: The Nerve Impulse: Action Potential
At rest, the inside of the axon membrane is negatively charged relative to outside (resting potential: −70 mV). This is maintained by the Na⁺/K⁺ pump (active transport: 3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in).

content.heading: Sequence of an action potential:
items: Stimulus reaches threshold (about −55 mV): voltage-gated Na⁺ channels open → Na⁺ rushes in → inside becomes positive (depolarisation, up to +40 mV).
Na⁺ channels close; voltage-gated K⁺ channels open → K⁺ rushes out → inside becomes negative again (repolarisation).
K⁺ channels close — slight overshoot below resting potential (hyperpolarisation / refractory period).
Na⁺/K⁺ pump restores original ion distribution — resting potential re-established.
The refractory period prevents the impulse travelling backwards — ensures one-way transmission.

content.heading: Factors affecting impulse speed:
items: Myelination: myelinated axons conduct much faster (up to 120 m/s) via saltatory conduction vs unmyelinated (~1 m/s).
Axon diameter: wider axons have less resistance — faster conduction.
Temperature: higher temperature = faster conduction (up to ~40°C). This explains why cold packs slow nerve conduction in pain management, and warm-ups before sport improve reaction times.

content.body: Chemical Synapses

A synapse is a junction between two neurones (or a neurone and an effector). Most synapses in the human nervous system are chemical synapses — they use neurotransmitters to transmit the signal across the synaptic cleft.

content.heading: Sequence of synaptic transmission:
items: Action potential arrives at presynaptic knob.
Voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels open → Ca²⁺ enters the knob.
Ca²⁺ triggers synaptic vesicles to fuse with presynaptic membrane → neurotransmitter (e.g. acetylcholine) released into synaptic cleft by exocytosis.
Neurotransmitter diffuses across the cleft and binds to specific receptors on the postsynaptic membrane.
Ion channels on postsynaptic membrane open → Na⁺ enters → depolarisation of postsynaptic membrane → new action potential generated (if threshold is reached).
Neurotransmitter is broken down by enzymes (acetylcholinesterase breaks down acetylcholine into choline + acetate) or is reabsorbed — prevents continuous stimulation.

content.heading: Excitatory vs Inhibitory synapses:
items: Excitatory synapses: neurotransmitter causes depolarisation of postsynaptic membrane → more likely to fire. Example: glutamate.

Inhibitory synapses: neurotransmitter causes hyperpolarisation (Cl⁻ enters or K⁺ leaves) → less likely to fire. Example: GABA (gamma-aminobutyric acid). Important for fine motor control and preventing seizures.

content.title: Local anaesthetics and nerve impulses
content.body: Local anaesthetics (e.g. lidocaine used by Ugandan dentists and surgeons) work by blocking voltage-gated Na⁺ channels. Without Na⁺ influx, no action potential can be generated. The nerve cannot transmit pain signals — the area becomes numb. The effect is reversible as the drug diffuses away.

questions.question: A patient with multiple sclerosis has demyelination (destruction of myelin sheaths) in their nervous system. The most likely effect on nerve impulse transmission is:
options: Faster impulse conduction due to more nodes of Ranvier
Slower, less reliable impulse conduction as saltatory conduction is disrupted
The impulse reverses direction along the axon
More neurotransmitter is released at synapses
questions.answer: Slower, less reliable impulse conduction as saltatory conduction is disrupted
questions.explanation: Myelin enables saltatory conduction — the impulse jumps rapidly between Nodes of Ranvier, greatly increasing speed (up to 120 m/s). Without myelin, impulse must travel along the entire membrane — much slower and may be blocked entirely. This explains the motor and sensory symptoms of multiple sclerosis.

questions.question: At a chemical synapse, calcium ions (Ca²⁺) enter the presynaptic knob when an action potential arrives. What is the direct role of Ca²⁺?
options: Opening Na⁺ channels on the postsynaptic membrane
Triggering vesicle fusion with the presynaptic membrane, releasing neurotransmitter
Breaking down acetylcholine in the synaptic cleft
Generating the action potential in the postsynaptic neurone
questions.answer: Triggering vesicle fusion with the presynaptic membrane, releasing neurotransmitter
questions.explanation: Ca²⁺ ions entering the presynaptic knob act as a second messenger. They bind to proteins on synaptic vesicles, causing them to move to and fuse with the presynaptic membrane. Neurotransmitter is then released by exocytosis into the synaptic cleft.

questions.question: Why does the refractory period of an axon ensure one-directional transmission of impulses?
options: Na⁺/K⁺ pumps only work in one direction
The section of membrane that just fired cannot fire again immediately — so the impulse can only move forward to resting membrane
Myelin prevents backward flow of ions
Dendrites prevent the impulse returning to the cell body
questions.answer: The section of membrane that just fired cannot fire again immediately — so the impulse can only move forward to resting membrane
questions.explanation: During the refractory period, voltage-gated Na⁺ channels are inactivated and cannot open. The membrane behind the impulse is in the refractory state and cannot depolarise again. The impulse can therefore only travel in one direction — toward unexcited membrane ahead.

questions.question: An athlete warms up before a race in Kampala. One reason warm-up improves reaction time is:
options: Warming up increases myelination of motor neurones
Higher muscle temperature increases nerve impulse conduction speed
Warm-up increases the number of synaptic vesicles
Temperature has no effect on nerve conduction
questions.answer: Higher muscle temperature increases nerve impulse conduction speed
questions.explanation: Higher temperature increases the kinetic energy of ions and speeds up the opening and closing of voltage-gated ion channels. This increases the speed of action potential propagation along nerve fibres — improving reaction time and coordination. This is a key physiological reason for warm-up routines.

lessons.title: Sensory Receptors, the Retina and the Ear

content.body: Properties of Sensory Receptors

items: Sensory receptors are transducers — they convert one form of energy (stimulus) into electrical energy (nerve impulse).
Specificity: each receptor type responds to only one type of stimulus (e.g. photoreceptors respond to light, not sound).
Generator potential: when a receptor is stimulated, it generates a small depolarisation (generator potential). If this reaches threshold, an action potential is triggered in the sensory neurone.
Adaptation: with prolonged stimulation, the frequency of action potentials decreases — the receptor 'gets used' to the stimulus (e.g. you stop smelling a familiar odour).

content.heading: Types of sensory receptors:
items: Mechanoreceptors: detect pressure, touch, sound (e.g. Pacinian corpuscles in skin, hair cells in the ear).
Thermoreceptors: detect temperature (skin).
Chemoreceptors: detect chemicals (taste buds, olfactory receptors, carotid body — detects blood CO₂).
Photoreceptors: detect light (rod and cone cells in retina).
Nociceptors: detect pain (free nerve endings throughout body).

content.body: The Retina: Photoreceptors and Visual Perception

content.heading: Rod cells:
items: Contain rhodopsin (visual purple) — a photosensitive pigment that bleaches in light, triggering a nerve impulse.
Highly sensitive — can detect single photons. Function in dim light (night vision).
Cannot detect colour — only light intensity.
Distributed mainly in the peripheral retina.
Many rods converge on a single bipolar cell (retinal convergence) — increases sensitivity but reduces acuity (sharpness).

content.heading: Cone cells:
items: Three types: red-sensitive, green-sensitive, blue-sensitive (different opsins — photopsin pigments).
Require high light intensity — function in bright light (day vision).
Allow colour discrimination.
Concentrated in the fovea (the point of sharpest vision, directly behind the lens).
Each cone connects to its own bipolar cell (no convergence) — high visual acuity (sharpness).

content.title: Why you see better in dim light by looking slightly to the side
content.body: The fovea contains only cones, which need bright light. When you look directly at something dim (e.g. a faint star), the image falls on the cone-rich fovea and you may not see it. Looking slightly to the side directs the image onto the rod-rich peripheral retina, which is much more sensitive in low light.
The Ear: Vestibular Apparatus and Balance
The inner ear contains the cochlea (hearing) and the vestibular apparatus (balance). The vestibular apparatus has two components:

items: Semicircular canals (3 canals at right angles to each other): detect ROTATIONAL movement (turning the head). Each canal contains endolymph fluid. Movement causes endolymph to flow past hair cells in the ampulla → hair cells bend → nerve impulses sent to brain.

Utricle and saccule (otolith organs): detect LINEAR acceleration and the position of the head relative to gravity. Contain otoliths (calcium carbonate crystals) resting on hair cells. When head tilts or accelerates, otoliths shift → hair cells bend → signals sent to brain.
Information from vestibular apparatus is integrated with visual signals and proprioceptors (joint/muscle sensors) by the cerebellum to maintain balance and posture.

questions.question: After sitting in a smelly room for 30 minutes, a student stops noticing the smell. This is an example of:
options: Sensory adaptation — the receptor reduces its response to a constant stimulus
The receptor being permanently damaged by the chemical
The brain stopping production of olfactory neurones
The generator potential exceeding the threshold permanently
questions.answer: Sensory adaptation — the receptor reduces its response to a constant stimulus
questions.explanation: Sensory adaptation occurs when receptors reduce their firing frequency in response to a prolonged, unchanging stimulus. The olfactory receptors initially respond strongly to the smell but adapt over time, sending fewer impulses to the brain — the smell is no longer consciously perceived.

questions.question: A person with a damaged fovea would most likely experience:
options: Complete blindness in both eyes
Loss of central, high-acuity colour vision
Loss of peripheral night vision
Inability to detect any light
questions.answer: Loss of central, high-acuity colour vision
questions.explanation: The fovea is densely packed with cone cells — it is the point of highest visual acuity and colour discrimination. Damage to the fovea causes loss of central, detailed, colour vision while peripheral (rod-based) vision may be preserved.

questions.question: Many rod cells converge onto a single bipolar cell in the retina. What is the consequence of this convergence?
options: Increased colour discrimination
Increased sensitivity in dim light at the cost of reduced visual acuity
Faster transmission of visual signals to the brain
Improved central vision in the fovea
questions.answer: Increased sensitivity in dim light at the cost of reduced visual acuity
questions.explanation: Retinal convergence means that stimulation of any one of many rods is sufficient to trigger the bipolar cell. Small amounts of light falling on a wide area of retina are summed — increasing sensitivity. However, the brain cannot determine which individual rod was stimulated — visual acuity (sharpness) is reduced.

questions.question: A passenger in a bus experiences motion sickness when reading. Motion sickness occurs because:
options: The cochlea is overstimulated by loud engine noise
There is a conflict between signals from the vestibular apparatus (detecting movement) and the eyes (focused on a stationary page)
Rod cells in the retina cannot function in moving vehicles
The semicircular canals are damaged by vibration
questions.answer: There is a conflict between signals from the vestibular apparatus (detecting movement) and the eyes (focused on a stationary page)
questions.explanation: When reading in a moving vehicle, the vestibular apparatus detects movement and acceleration, but the eyes see a stationary page and send signals indicating no movement. This sensory conflict confuses the brain (cerebellum), causing nausea and motion sickness.

content.body: Types of Animal Behaviour

Animal behaviour is any observable action or response of an organism. Behaviour evolved because it increases survival and reproductive success. It ranges from simple reflex actions to complex learned behaviours.
Innate (Instinctive) Behaviour
Innate behaviour is inherited, present from birth, and does not require learning or experience. It is stereotyped — always performed the same way by all individuals of a species.

content.heading: Types of innate behaviour:
items: Reflex actions: automatic, rapid responses to stimuli (e.g. withdrawing hand from heat, blinking, knee-jerk). Mediated by a reflex arc — sensory neurone → relay neurone → motor neurone. No conscious processing required.

Taxes (taxis): directional movement toward (positive) or away from (negative) a stimulus. Example: positive phototaxis in moths (fly toward light); negative phototaxis in cockroaches (move away from light). Maggots show negative phototaxis (burrow away from light into decaying matter).

Kineses: non-directional change in speed or turning rate in response to stimulus intensity. Example: woodlice move faster and turn more frequently in dry conditions (increasing chance of finding damp — but not directed toward it).

content.body: Learned Behaviour
Learned behaviour is acquired through experience and can be modified. It is more flexible than innate behaviour and allows animals to adapt to changing environments.

content.heading: Types of learned behaviour:
items: Habituation: decreased response to a repeated harmless stimulus. Example: birds near a busy Kampala road stop flying away at vehicle noise. Simple nervous system adaptation — no reward or punishment involved.

Classical conditioning (Pavlovian): association of a new (conditioned) stimulus with a natural (unconditioned) stimulus. Example: Pavlov's dogs salivated to a bell (conditioned) after the bell was repeatedly paired with food (unconditioned).

Operant conditioning (trial and error): animal learns to associate a behaviour with a reward or punishment. Example: a rat learns to press a lever to receive food. Important in animal training and in human learning.

Imprinting: rapid, irreversible learning during a sensitive period early in life. Example: ducklings follow the first moving object they see after hatching (Lorenz's greylag geese). Critical for parent-offspring bonding.

Insight learning (reasoning): solving a new problem by applying past experience without trial and error. Considered the highest form of learned behaviour. Observed in great apes (chimpanzees using sticks to reach food), crows, and humans.

content.heading: Evolutionary significance of behaviour:
items: Innate behaviour: reliable in stable, predictable environments. No learning time required — adaptive at birth.
Learned behaviour: more flexible — adaptive in changing environments.
Migration (e.g. East African wildebeest, Ugandan birds): complex behaviour combining innate navigation ability with learned experience — ensures food and breeding success.
Mating displays: attract mates and signal genetic fitness — increase reproductive success (e.g. male birds-of-paradise, Uganda's grey crowned crane displays).

questions.question: Maggots move away from light in a directed manner. This behaviour is classified as:
options: Positive phototaxis
Negative phototaxis
Kinesis
Habituation
questions.explanation: A taxis is a directed movement toward (positive) or away from (negative) a stimulus. Since maggots move directionally away from light, this is negative phototaxis. This is innate behaviour that increases survival by directing maggots into dark, moist, food-rich environments.

questions.question: A dog trainer repeatedly pairs a whistle with giving a dog a treat, until the dog salivates at the whistle alone. This is an example of:
options: Operant conditioning
Habituation
Classical conditioning
Insight learning
questions.explanation: Classical conditioning (Pavlovian conditioning) involves pairing a neutral (conditioned) stimulus (whistle) with a stimulus that naturally produces a response (treat → salivation). After repeated pairings, the conditioned stimulus alone triggers the response (salivation at the whistle).

questions.question: Birds nesting near a busy road in Kampala no longer fly away when vehicles pass. This is an example of:
options: Imprinting during a sensitive period
Habituation to a repeated harmless stimulus
Classical conditioning to vehicle sounds
Insight learning from experience
questions.answer: Habituation to a repeated harmless stimulus
questions.explanation: Habituation is the simplest form of learning — a decrease in response to a stimulus that is repeated but has no significant consequence (no reward or punishment). The birds learn that passing vehicles are harmless and reduce their escape response, saving energy.
